aliases: 罐頭鮑魚 (gun3 tau4 baau1 jyu4)
डिब्बाबंद एबलोन (चीनी शैली में पकाया हुआ)

chinese: 罐頭鮑魚 (gun3 tau4 baau1 jyu4)
hindi: डिब्बाबंद एबलोन (चीनी शैली में पकाया हुआ)

region_of_origin: Southern Australia and South Africa (primary canned abalone sources); consumed as premium prestige dish in Singapore
food_subgroup: Canned shellfish — abalone (Haliotis spp.), drained from brine, further braised with oyster sauce, abalone sauce, and aromatics

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FOODON_03411433 (abalone food product) is the closest defensible FoodOn parent. No FoodOn URI exists specifically for canned braised abalone in Chinese style. The URI is used as a closest-parent anchor only; the canned and braised preparation specifics are not captured by this term.
ontology.foodex2_description: Abalone (Haliotis spp.) — closest FoodEx2 parent for canned mollusc/shellfish product. Code A0CJW corresponds to abalone in the FoodEx2 catalogue. Further braised preparation with Chinese sauces is not captured by this code; used as closest-parent anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for canned braised abalone in Chinese style. A0CJW (abalone) used as closest-parent anchor. Canning, brine, and braising sauce preparation details are not encoded in this term.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code for canned braised abalone. Not assigned to avoid fabrication.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving is 2 pieces of canned abalone (approximately 80g drained weight), typically served as a braised CNY dish with oyster sauce, mushrooms, and broccoli.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Abalone, mixed species, raw (FDC ID 175159) and USDA Branded Foods canned abalone entries; cross-referenced with Singapore HPB Foodo database for canned shellfish
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values for canned braised abalone are adapted from USDA FoodData Central abalone (raw and canned) entries and USDA Branded Foods canned abalone products, adjusted for canning brine (sodium increase) and braising sauce contribution (additional sodium, minor carbohydrate). Singapore HPB Foodo consulted for regional context. Values represent the canned drained product before further braising sauce addition.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of mixed shellfish and canned seafood products — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for canned seafood with added starch/brine; protein-dominant foods with minor starch contribution
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for canned braised abalone. GI ~42 (LOW) is an analogous estimate based on: (1) canned seafood products with added starch in brine from the University of Sydney GI Database; (2) the protein-dominant macronutrient profile of abalone (18g protein/100g) which substantially dampens glucose response; (3) low total carbohydrate content (6g/100g, primarily from brine starch and minor oyster sauce contribution). GL per 80g serving = (42 × 4.8g available carb) / 100 = ~2.0, rounded to 3.0 to account for braising sauce carbohydrate contribution. Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium from canning brine (~420mg/100g) plus additional sodium from braising sauces (oyster sauce, abalone sauce)
hypertension.guidance: A 2-piece (80g) serving contributes approximately 336mg sodium from the canned product alone; when further braised with oyster sauce and abalone sauce, total sodium per serving may reach 640–900mg, representing 28–39% of the 2,300mg daily limit, so hypertensive individuals should limit portion size, request reduced-sauce preparation, and avoid consuming the braising liquid.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~42) and low glycemic load (~3 per 80g serving); high protein content (18g/100g) dampens postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Canned braised abalone has a low GI (~42) and low GL (~3 per 80g serving), making it a suitable protein-rich choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; however, the sodium content from braising sauces warrants attention if the patient also has hypertension, and portion control of accompanying starchy sides (rice, noodles) remains important.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.2g/100g) and low total fat (0.8g/100g) are cardioprotective; however, elevated sodium from brine and braising sauces is a cardiovascular risk factor
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The lipid profile of canned abalone is highly favourable for cardiovascular health (0.8g total fat, 0.2g saturated fat, 0g trans fat per 100g), and selenium (44mcg/100g) and vitamin E (3.4mg/100g) provide antioxidant support; the primary cardiovascular concern is cumulative sodium intake from brine and braising sauces, which should be managed by limiting sauce consumption and balancing with low-sodium foods throughout the day.

gout_hyperuricemia.key_factor: Low purine content (~50–80mg/100g) — exceptionally low for a shellfish
gout_hyperuricemia.guidance: Canned abalone is safe for individuals with gout or hyperuricaemia as its purine content (~50–80mg/100g) is substantially lower than most shellfish and falls within the low-purine category; it can be consumed in moderate portions without triggering uric acid elevation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High selenium (44mcg/100g, ~80% daily AI) and vitamin E (3.4mg/100g) provide antioxidant and anti-inflammatory support
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Canned abalone is a beneficial food for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its high selenium content (44mcg/100g, approximately 80% of the daily adequate intake) and vitamin E (3.4mg/100g), both of which support antioxidant defence and membrane protection relevant to RA pathophysiology.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Canned braised abalone (Pacific/Australian, Haliotis laevigata or H. rubra) is a premium prestige shellfish product consumed widely in Singapore as a centrepiece dish at Chinese New Year reunion dinners and Cantonese banquets. The product is abalone canned in brine (water, salt, starch), drained, and further braised with oyster sauce, abalone sauce, mushrooms, and broccoli. The texture is tender and slightly chewy with a mild, oceanic umami flavour intensified by the braising sauce. Per 100g (drained, before additional braising sauce): 105 kcal, 18g protein, 0.8g fat (0.2g saturated), 6g carbohydrate, 1g sugar, 0g dietary fibre, 420mg sodium. A standard 2-piece serving (~80g) provides approximately 84 kcal, 14.4g protein, 0.64g fat, 4.8g carbohydrate, and 336mg sodium from the canned product alone (braising sauce adds further sodium). Nutritional highlights: selenium 44mcg/100g (~80% daily AI — among the highest of common shellfish in Singapore), vitamin E 3.4mg/100g, iron 2.5mg/100g, phosphorus 220mg/100g, magnesium 48mg/100g. GI ~42 (LOW); GL ~3 per serving. Exceptionally low purine content (~50–80mg/100g) makes it safe for gout patients despite being a shellfish. Sodium is the primary clinical concern due to brine and braising sauces. Popular brands in Singapore: New Moon (South Africa), Skylight (South Africa/Australia), Emperor (Australia). Dietary flags: halal, dairy-free. Source: USDA FoodData Central + Singapore HPB Foodo.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central abalone entries (raw and canned) and USDA Branded Foods canned abalone products, cross-referenced with Singapore HPB Foodo for regional context. The macronutrient profile is consistent across multiple approved sources. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework with documented basis. Ontology anchors use FOODON_03411433 (abalone food product) and FoodEx2 code A0CJW (abalone) as closest-parent references, both defensible and documented with gap notes. Confidence is high given strong source alignment and well-characterised food type.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for canned braised abalone is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of April 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for canned drained abalone with braising sauce not reported in available approved sources. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for abalone). vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 (confirmed negligible/zero for canned abalone per USDA FoodData Central). vitamin_a_mcg_rae is 0.0 (confirmed negligible for canned abalone per USDA FoodData Central). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed negligible for canned abalone per USDA FoodData Central). dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 (naturally zero — pure shellfish product with no plant fibre). The input record description field contained an erroneous description referencing pork liver sausage; this has been corrected to accurately describe canned braised abalone. Sodium values reflect the canned drained product; actual sodium per serving will be higher when braising sauces are included. GI ~42 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for canned braised abalone exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of April 2026. Ontology: FoodOn URI FOODON_03411433 (abalone food product) and FoodEx2 code A0CJW (abalone) are used as closest-parent anchors; neither captures the canned and braised preparation specifics, as documented in gap notes.
